summary: Today's sweep leans toward Democracy / press freedom, CS education / AI policy, Education / pedagogy. The strongest items in this run are Standard-Based Grading Offers a Different Model of Assessing Student Learning, Singer D4vd charged with murder of 14-year-old girl found in car, and Fortnite developers can make AI characters now — just don’t try to date them.

mainItems.title: Standard-Based Grading Offers a Different Model of Assessing Student Learning
mainItems.summary: Some school districts, including ones in Maine, New Mexico, Iowa and Oregon, are shifting to standards-based grading, where students are graded on the skills and concepts they learn instead of points accumulated from assignments and tests…
mainItems.whyItMatters: Directly relevant to teaching, student agency, and school policy.

mainItems.title: Singer D4vd charged with murder of 14-year-old girl found in car
mainItems.summary: Musician has been charged after the dismembered and decomposing body of Celeste Rivas Hernandez was found in abandoned Tesla The singer D4vd has been charged with the murder of Celeste Rivas Hernandez , the teenage girl whose dismembered…
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to state power, civil liberties, and anti-authoritarian concerns.

mainItems.title: Fortnite developers can make AI characters now — just don’t try to date them
mainItems.summary: Following last year's AI-powered Darth Vader in Fortnite that swore in a re-creation of James Earl Jones' voice, Epic Games is now letting Fortnite creators experiment with a new "conversations" tool to create AI-powered characters that…
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to state power, civil liberties, and anti-authoritarian concerns.

mainItems.title: California Students Author New ‘Digital Wellness’ Bill, Say Phone Bans Fall Short
mainItems.summary: After taking a break from social media, Orange County student Elise Choi helped write a bill that would mandate California schools teach digital wellness — a response to growing concerns about how technology is affecting students’ mental…
mainItems.whyItMatters: Directly relevant to teaching, classroom practice, and educational judgment.

mainItems.title: Humanoid ‘Lightning’ robot smashes the half-marathon record
mainItems.summary: An autonomous scarlet robot named "Lightning" finished a 13-mile race in Beijing on Sunday in just 50 minutes and 26 seconds, less than half the time of last year's fastest robot, which took two hours and 40 minutes.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to the shape of current AI systems and where the tooling is moving.

mainItems.title: My family tried to eat fewer ultra-processed foods for five years. Here’s what we learned
mainItems.summary: Cutting UPFs from our grocery list was expensive, laborious and time-consuming Grocery shopping looks different these days.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to state power, civil liberties, and anti-authoritarian concerns.

mainItems.title: This week in PC games: the "final" Molyneux game, ski resort management, Game Boy horror and a glamcore heist sim
mainItems.summary: Happy this week, all! I return from my holiday in the mountains with spirit restored, skin bronzed, and knees destroyed and reforged into coiled springs of purest Adventure.

mainItems.whyItMatters: Touches trust, surveillance, and the private use of AI systems.

mainItems.summary: Official tells Reuters it is ‘positively reviewing’ involvement in negotiations after earlier saying it had no plans for a new round of talks The US has just released some more footage of the encounter with the Iranian flagged vessel, the…

mainItems.title: A humanoid robot sprints past the human half-marathon world record in Beijing race

new.title: Scavengers Reign
new.why: Animated speculative sci-fi with strong worldbuilding and a weirder imagination than most prestige SF.

new.title: Sugar
new.why: Noir detective structure with a genre wrinkle and a sensibility that feels adjacent to your crime/strangeness overlap.

new.title: The Lazarus Project
new.why: Time-loop espionage, moral pressure, and speculative plotting — very plausible fit.

new.title: Bodies
new.why: Time-spanning conspiracy/mystery structure that fits your taste for long-arc puzzle storytelling.

older.title: Counterpart
older.why: Probably the single cleanest “James show you may have missed”: espionage + parallel-world sci-fi + adult competence.

older.title: Patriot
older.why: Dry, strange, melancholy spy storytelling with a very distinct voice.

older.title: Utopia
older.why: Paranoid conspiracy energy, formal boldness, and a willingness to get strange and ugly.

older.title: Rubicon
older.why: A low-key conspiracy thriller that leans heavily on intelligence work, paranoia, and institutional atmosphere.

shared.title: Bad Sisters
shared.why: Darkly funny, well-observed, and ensemble-driven in a way that plausibly overlaps with Hacks / Derry Girls / Mare of Easttown energy.

shared.title: Detectorists
shared.why: Gentle, funny, humane, and specific — a softer shared-watch possibility.

shared.title: Astrid et Raphaëlle
shared.why: French procedural with a strong central duo and exactly the kind of foreign-crime appeal Maggie may share with you.

shared.title: Somebody Somewhere
shared.why: A warmer character-driven recommendation if the shared overlap leans more Bear / Good Place than pure detective work.

highlights.title: A.I. Is Prompting an Evolution, Not Extinction, for Coders
highlights.notes: Interesting piece on the current state of AI in software work.

highlights.title: The Workers Letting A.I. Do Their Jobs
highlights.notes: In an era of agents powered by artificial intelligence, many programmers are barely programming.

highlights.title: Trump’s presidency is what evil looks like: absurd, frightening, cruel
highlights.notes: Particularly strong Guardian opinion piece, noted for future briefing highlights.
